 `./.tmp/xero-refs/xero-command-line/README.md`

```text
1	# xero-command-line
2	
3	A command-line tool for the Xero API using PKCE OAuth. Authenticates via browser-based login — no client secret required. Supports multiple Xero organisations via named profiles.
4	
5	> **Alpha** (v0.0.7) — APIs may change.
6	
7	## Install
8	
9	```bash
10	npm install -g @xeroapi/xero-command-line
11	```
12	
13	Or try it without installing:
14	
15	```bash
16	npx @xeroapi/xero-command-line <command>
17	```
18	
19	### From source
20	
21	```bash
22	git clone https://github.com/XeroAPI/xero-command-line.git
23	cd xero-command-line
24	npm install
25	npm run build
26	npm link
27	```
28	
29	### Prerequisites
30	
31	- Node.js >= 18
32	- A Xero OAuth 2.0 app at [developer.xero.com](https://developer.xero.com/app/manage) (no client secret needed — PKCE handles authentication)
33	- Set the redirect URI on your OAuth 2.0 PKCE app to `http://localhost:8742/callback`
34	
35	## Setup
36	
37	1. **Create a profile** with your Xero app's Client ID:
38	
39	```bash
40	xero profile add my-company
41	# Prompts for Client ID
42	```
43	
44	Or pass it inline:
45	
46	```bash
47	xero profile add my-company --client-id YOUR_CLIENT_ID
48	```
49	
50	2. **Log in** via the browser:
51	
52	```bash
53	xero login
54	```
55	
56	This opens your browser for Xero's OAuth consent flow. After you authorize, the CLI receives tokens via a local callback server. If your app is connected to multiple organisations, you'll be prompted to select one.
57	
58	Tokens are encrypted (AES-256-GCM) and cached at `~/.config/xero-command-line/tokens.json`. Tokens refresh automatically when expired.
59	
60	### Token storage
61	
62	The CLI encrypts OAuth tokens at rest in `~/.config/xero-command-line/tokens.json`. Where the **encryption key** is stored, from most to least secure:
63	
64	1. **OS keychain (default in `auto` mode)** — macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, or Linux Secret Service (GNOME Keyring / KWallet via D-Bus). Recommended on desktop systems. No encryption key file is written unless you opt in below.
65	2. **`XERO_TOKEN_PASSPHRASE`** — Derives the key with scrypt from your passphrase and a local salt file. The raw key is not stored on disk. Set the same value before `xero login` and every later command. Stronger than a file-stored key if you accept managing a secret env var.
66	3. **`XERO_KEYRING_FILE_BACKUP=1` (opt-in)** — When the keychain accepts a new key, also writes a copy to `~/.config/xero-command-line/.encryption-key` (mode `0600`). If a later command cannot read the keychain (common on **WSL**, **SSH**, or **headless** Linux), the CLI reads this file instead. **Off by default** so macOS/Windows/Linux desktops keep the key keychain-only. Enable only when the keychain is installed but reads are unreliable.
67	4. **`XERO_KEY_STORAGE=file`** — Store the key only in `.encryption-key` (skip the keychain). Use when no secret service is available; weaker than keychain-only because any process running as your user can read the key file.
68	
69	| Variable | Values | Default |
70	|----------|--------|---------|
71	| `XERO_KEY_STORAGE` | `auto`, `keyring`, `file` | `auto` |
72	| `XERO_KEYRING_FILE_BACKUP` | `1`, `true`, `yes` | off |
73	| `XERO_TOKEN_PASSPHRASE` | your passphrase | off |
74	
75	In `auto` mode without file backup, if the keychain is completely unavailable at login, the CLI still writes `.encryption-key` once so login can succeed — same as a file-only fallback for that session.
76	
77	On Linux, WSL, or SSH, if login works once and later commands fail with an encryption-key error, the secret service was likely unavailable in that shell. The CLI does **not** delete `tokens.json` on decrypt errors.
78	
79	**WSL / headless Linux (recommended: fix the keychain):**
80	
81	```bash
82	sudo apt install -y gnome-keyring libsecret-1-0 libsecret-tools dbus-x11
83	
84	# Add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
85	if [ -z "$GNOME_KEYRING_CONTROL" ]; then
86	  eval "$(/usr/bin/gnome-keyring-daemon --start --components=secrets 2>/dev/null)"
87	fi
88	```
89	
90	Verify: `secret-tool lookup service xero-command-line username encryption-key` (after `xero login`).
91	
92	**WSL with a flaky keychain (login works, later commands fail):**
93	
94	```bash
95	export XERO_KEYRING_FILE_BACKUP=1   # add to ~/.bashrc on that machine
96	xero login
97	```
98	
99	**Without any keychain** — use one of:
100	
101	```bash
102	export XERO_KEY_STORAGE=file
103	# or
104	export XERO_TOKEN_PASSPHRASE='your-secret'
105	xero login
106	```
107	
108	### OAuth scopes
109	
110	By default, `xero login` requests a broad set of scopes suitable for full read/write CLI usage. To request a narrower scope set — for example a read-only integration — pass `--scope` with a space-separated list of API scopes:
111	
112	```bash
113	xero login --scope "accounting.contacts.read accounting.settings.read accounting.invoices.read accounting.payments.read accounting.banktransactions.read accounting.manualjournals.read accounting.reports.balancesheet.read accounting.reports.profitandloss.read accounting.reports.trialbalance.read accounting.reports.aged.read accounting.budgets.read accounting.attachments.read"
114	```
115	
116	Or set the `XERO_SCOPES` environment variable (same space-separated format):
117	
118	```bash
119	XERO_SCOPES="accounting.invoices.read accounting.contacts.read" xero login
120	```
121	API credentials created before [Xero's March 2026 scope changes](https://developer.xero.com/faq/granular-scopes) where deprecated broader scopes like `accounting.transactions` and `accounting.reports` are available until September 2027 may request those scopes via the `--scope` override as well. `xero login` will request the granular scopes that replaced these deprecated broader scopes by default.
122	
123	Required OAuth scopes (`openid`, `profile`, `email`, `offline_access`) are always prepended automatically — you only need to list the Xero API scopes your app supports. If you omit `--scope`, behaviour is unchanged from previous releases.
124	
125	Re-run `xero login` (with or without `--scope`) whenever you change scopes; existing tokens retain the scopes granted at login time.
126	
127	## Global Flags
128	
129	Every command that calls the Xero API supports:
130	
131	| Flag | Description |
132	|---|---|
133	| `-p, --profile <name>` | Use a specific profile (defaults to the default profile) |
134	| `--client-id <id>` | Override profile with an inline client ID |
135	| `--json` | Output raw JSON (for piping/scripting) |
136	| `--csv` | Output as CSV |
137	| `--toon` | Output as [TOON](https://github.com/toon-format/toon) (compact, LLM-friendly) |
138	
139	Environment variables `XERO_PROFILE` and `XERO_CLIENT_ID` are also supported. Token storage can be tuned with `XERO_KEY_STORAGE`, `XERO_KEYRING_FILE_BACKUP`, and `XERO_TOKEN_PASSPHRASE` (see [Token storage](#token-storage)). The `xero login` command additionally accepts `XERO_SCOPES` (see [OAuth scopes](#oauth-scopes) above).
140	
141	## Finding IDs
142	
143	Most commands that reference contacts, invoices, or accounts require a Xero GUID (e.g., `edc74793-8d7e-4bf2-9e63-146dc4c675a2`). Use the list commands to find IDs:
144	
145	```bash
146	xero contacts list --search "Acme"   # find a contact ID
147	xero accounts list                    # find account codes
148	xero invoices list                    # find invoice IDs
149	```
150	
151	## Commands
152	
153	### Profile Management
154	
155	```bash
156	# Add a profile (first profile becomes the default)
157	xero profile add acme-corp
158	
159	# List all profiles
160	xero profile list
161	
162	# Set the default profile
163	xero profile set-default acme-corp
164	
165	# Remove a profile
166	xero profile remove acme-corp
167	```
168	
169	### Organisation
170	
171	```bash
172	xero org details
173	xero org details --json
174	```
175	
176	### Contacts
177	
178	```bash
179	# List contacts
180	xero contacts list
181	xero contacts list --search "Acme"
182	xero contacts list --page 2 --json
183	
184	# Create a contact
185	xero contacts create --name "Acme Corp" --email acme@example.com --phone "+1234567890"
186	xero contacts create --file contact.json
187	
188	# Update a contact
189	xero contacts update --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --name "Acme Corporation" --email new@acme.com
190	xero contacts update --file contact-update.json
191	```
192	
193	### Contact Groups
194	
195	```bash
196	xero contact-groups list
197	xero contact-groups list --group-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001
198	```
199	
200	### Accounts
201	
202	```bash
203	xero accounts list
204	xero accounts list --json
205	```
206	
207	### Invoices
208	
209	```bash
210	# List invoices
211	xero invoices list
212	xero invoices list --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001
213	xero invoices list --invoice-number INV-0001
214	xero invoices list --page 2 --csv
215	
216	# Create an invoice (inline flags for a single line item)
217	xero invoices create --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --type ACCREC \
218	  --description "Consulting" --quantity 10 --unit-amount 150 \
219	  --account-code 200 --tax-type OUTPUT2
220	
221	# Create an invoice (JSON file for multiple line items)
222	xero invoices create --file invoice.json
223	
224	# Update a draft invoice
225	xero invoices update --invoice-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --reference "Updated ref"
226	xero invoices update --file invoice-update.json
227	```
228	
229	<details>
230	<summary>Example invoice.json</summary>
231	
232	```json
233	{
234	  "contactId": "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001",
235	  "type": "ACCREC",
236	  "date": "2025-06-15",
237	  "reference": "REF-001",
238	  "lineItems": [
239	    {
240	      "description": "Consulting",
241	      "quantity": 10,
242	      "unitAmount": 150,
243	      "accountCode": "200",
244	      "taxType": "OUTPUT2"
245	    },
246	    {
247	      "description": "Travel",
248	      "quantity": 1,
249	      "unitAmount": 500,
250	      "accountCode": "200",
251	      "taxType": "OUTPUT2"
252	    }
253	  ]
254	}
255	```
256	
257	</details>
258	
259	### Quotes
260	
261	```bash
262	# List quotes
263	xero quotes list
264	xero quotes list --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001
265	xero quotes list --quote-number QU-0001
266	
267	# Create a quote
268	xero quotes create --file quote.json
269	xero quotes create --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --title "Project Quote" \
270	  --date 2025-12-30 --description "Web design" --quantity 1 --unit-amount 5000 \
271	  --account-code 200 --tax-type OUTPUT2
272	
273	# Update a draft quote
274	xero quotes update --file quote-update.json
275	```
276	
277	### Credit Notes
278	
279	```bash
280	# List credit notes
281	xero credit-notes list
282	xero credit-notes list --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --page 2
283	
284	# Create a credit note
285	xero credit-notes create --file credit-note.json
286	xero credit-notes create --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 \
287	  --description "Refund" --quantity 1 --unit-amount 100 \
288	  --account-code 200 --tax-type OUTPUT2
289	
290	# Update a draft credit note
291	xero credit-notes update --file credit-note-update.json
292	```
293	
294	### Manual Journals
295	
296	```bash
297	# List manual journals
298	xero manual-journals list
299	xero manual-journals list --manual-journal-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001
300	xero manual-journals list --modified-after 2025-01-01
301	
302	# Create a manual journal (requires JSON file — minimum 2 lines)
303	xero manual-journals create --file journal.json
304	
305	# Update a draft manual journal
306	xero manual-journals update --file journal-update.json
307	```
308	
309	<details>
310	<summary>Example journal.json</summary>
311	
312	```json
313	{
314	  "narration": "Reclassify office supplies",
315	  "manualJournalLines": [
316	    { "accountCode": "200", "lineAmount": 100, "description": "Debit" },
317	    { "accountCode": "400", "lineAmount": -100, "description": "Credit" }
318	  ]
319	}
320	```
321	
322	</details>
323	
324	### Bank Transactions
325	
326	```bash
327	# List bank transactions
328	xero bank-transactions list
329	xero bank-transactions list --bank-account-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001
330	
331	# Create a bank transaction
332	xero bank-transactions create --file bank-transaction.json
333	xero bank-transactions create --type SPEND --bank-account-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 \
334	  --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002 --description "Office supplies" \
335	  --quantity 1 --unit-amount 50 --account-code 429 --tax-type INPUT2
336	
337	# Update a bank transaction
338	xero bank-transactions update --file bank-transaction-update.json
339	```
340	
341	### Payments
342	
343	```bash
344	# List payments
345	xero payments list
346	xero payments list --invoice-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001
347	xero payments list --invoice-number INV-0001
348	xero payments list --reference "Payment ref"
349	
350	# Create a payment
351	xero payments create --invoice-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --account-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002 --amount 500
352	xero payments create --file payment.json
353	```
354	
355	### Items
356	
357	```bash
358	# List items
359	xero items list
360	xero items list --page 2
361	
362	# Create an item
363	xero items create --code WIDGET --name "Widget" --sale-price 29.99
364	xero items create --file item.json
365	
366	# Update an item
367	xero items update --item-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --code WIDGET --name "Updated Widget"
368	xero items update --file item-update.json
369	```
370	
371	### Currencies
372	
373	```bash
374	xero currencies list
375	xero currencies list --json
376	```
377	
378	### Tax Rates
379	
380	```bash
381	xero tax-rates list
382	xero tax-rates list --json
383	```
384	
385	### Tracking Categories & Options
386	
387	```bash
388	# List tracking categories
389	xero tracking categories list
390	xero tracking categories list --include-archived
391	
392	# Create a tracking category
393	xero tracking categories create --name "Department"
394	
395	# Update a tracking category
396	xero tracking categories update --category-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --name "Region"
397	xero tracking categories update --category-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --status ARCHIVED
398	
399	# Create tracking options
400	xero tracking options create --category-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --names "Sales,Marketing,Engineering"
401	
402	# Update tracking options
403	xero tracking options update --category-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --file tracking-options.json
404	```
405	
406	### Reports
407	
408	```bash
409	# Trial balance
410	xero reports trial-balance
411	xero reports trial-balance --date 2025-12-31 --json
412	
413	# Profit and loss
414	xero reports profit-and-loss
415	xero reports profit-and-loss --from 2025-01-01 --to 2025-12-31
416	xero reports profit-and-loss --timeframe QUARTER --periods 4
417	
418	# Balance sheet
419	xero reports balance-sheet
420	xero reports balance-sheet --date 2025-12-31
421	xero reports balance-sheet --timeframe MONTH --periods 12
422	
423	# Aged receivables (requires contact ID)
424	xero reports aged-receivables --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001
425	xero reports aged-receivables --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --report-date 2025-12-31
426	
427	# Aged payables (requires contact ID)
428	xero reports aged-payables --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001
429	xero reports aged-payables --contact-id 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 --from-date 2025-01-01 --to-date 2025-12-31
430	```
431	
432	## JSON File Input
433	
434	Commands that create or update resources accept a `--file` flag with a JSON payload. All inputs are validated before being sent to the API. Validation errors are displayed with specific field-level messages.
435	
436	```bash
437	xero invoices create --file invoice.json
438	```
439	
440	## Output Formats
441	
442	```bash
443	# Formatted table (default)
444	xero contacts list
445	
446	# JSON (for piping to jq, scripts, etc.)
447	xero contacts list --json
448	
449	# CSV (for spreadsheets)
450	xero contacts list --csv
451	
452	# TOON (compact format, ~40% fewer tokens — ideal for LLM pipelines)
453	xero contacts list --toon
454	```
455	
456	## Multiple Organisations
457	
458	Use profiles to manage multiple Xero orgs:
459	
460	```bash
461	xero profile add client-a --client-id ID_A
462	xero profile add client-b --client-id ID_B
463	
464	xero login -p client-a
465	xero login -p client-b
466	
467	xero contacts list --profile client-a
468	xero invoices list --profile client-b
469	```
470	
471	## Development
472	
473	```bash
474	# Run in dev mode (no build step)
475	npm run dev -- contacts list
476	
477	# Build
478	npm run build
479	
480	# Run tests
481	npm test
482	npm run test:watch
483	```
484
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./.tmp/xero-refs/xero-prompt-library/lovable/xero-integration-multi-account/SKILL.md`

```text
1	---
2	name: xero-integration-multi-account
3	description: Multi-tenant Xero on Lovable Cloud (TanStack Start + Lovable Cloud Postgres) — each customer connects their own Xero organisation and the app acts as that customer. Covers OAuth 2 (authorize → callback → refresh) with rotating refresh tokens, per-user encrypted token storage, scope selection (granular > broad), idempotent writes, sibling-row refresh, reconnect UX, and the Lovable-specific traps around iframe preview, host mapping, and redirect URIs.
4	---
5	
6	# Multi-account / multi-tenant Xero (each user connects their own Xero)
7	
8	This skill is for a **multi-tenant SaaS built in Lovable**: every customer links
9	**their own** Xero organisation, and your app reads/writes Xero **as that
10	customer**, with **their** tokens.
11	
12	> **First, make sure this is the right variant.** Lovable has no managed
13	> single-principal Xero connector, so you own the OAuth lifecycle either way.
14	> The deciding question is *how many Xero logins exist*:
15	>
16	> - **One — yours.** Internal dashboard, single-merchant store, back-office tool,
17	>   prototype. Stop and use **`xero-integration-single-account`** instead — one
18	>   stored token set, no per-user keying.
19	> - **Many — one per customer.** Each end user links *their own* Xero, and the
20	>   app acts as that customer. **This skill.** Per-user token rows, tenant
21	>   selection, multi-tenant refresh rotation.
22	>
23	> Don't confuse Xero **multi-org** with **multi-tenant**. `GET /connections`
24	> returns several *organisations* under ONE login — that's still one principal,
25	> still single-account. Multi-tenant = many *separate* Xero logins, one per
26	> customer.
27	
28	## Where this code runs in Lovable
29	
30	Lovable's current default stack is **TanStack Start** on a Cloudflare Workers-style
31	serverless runtime, with **Lovable Cloud** for Postgres, auth, secrets, and deployment.
32	Lovable Cloud runs on Supabase under the hood, so the auto-generated env variables and
33	imports still say `supabase` (e.g. `@/integrations/supabase/client.server`). App-internal
34	server logic — including all Xero OAuth and token handling — lives in **`createServerFn`
35	server functions**; external callers (Xero's browser redirect, webhooks, cron) hit
36	**server routes** under `src/routes/api/public/*`. **Do not use Supabase Edge Functions**
37	for any of this.
38	
39	Two runtime facts shape the code below:
40	
41	- The server runtime is stateless across invocations, so the OAuth `state` value
42	  must be persisted to a table between the connect step and the callback.
43	- Use `fetch` for the OAuth lifecycle (authorize redirect, token exchange, refresh)
44	  — three calls, no SDK needed. You *may* use `npm:xero-node` for read/write API
45	  calls if it bundles cleanly, but keep the OAuth flow `fetch`-based.
46	
47	**Crypto is Node `crypto`** (the Worker runtime supports it via `nodejs_compat`);
48	the three-part `iv.tag.enc` ciphertext shape below is correct on this stack.
49	
50	> **If you're working in a legacy Lovable project that still uses Supabase Edge
51	> Functions (Deno) directly as its server layer**, port these handlers to Edge Functions:
52	> start/callback/getToken become Edge Functions, secrets are read via
53	> `Deno.env.get` (and you must redeploy after changing one), the publicly-reachable
54	> callback needs `verify_jwt = false`, and crypto becomes Web Crypto with a
55	> two-part `iv.ciphertext` blob. Everything else carries over unchanged.
56	
57	## Decision check before building
58	
59	Build the multi-tenant version only if ALL are true:
60	- Each customer connects their **own** Xero org (not yours).
61	- The app acts under **each customer's** Xero identity, not a shared one.
62	- You can securely store and rotate per-user refresh tokens (Lovable Cloud Postgres + RLS, below).
63	
64	If any is false, use `xero-integration-single-account` (one stored token set).
65	
66	---
67	
68	## ⚠️ Pre-flight — do this FIRST (skipping it costs hours)
69	
70	Three checks. Each one prevents a class of bug that produces a misleading
71	upstream error from Xero. Do them **before** you wire the Connect button to a
72	human.
73	
74	### 1. Assert every secret is set and non-empty
75	
76	Empty/missing secrets surface as `unauthorized_client` from Xero (because
77	`client_id=undefined` got sent), which points away from the real cause. Fail fast
78	with a clear message inside `xeroStart` **before** building the URL:
79	
80	```ts
81	function requireEnv(name: string) {
82	  const v = process.env[name];
83	  if (!v || v.trim() === "") {
84	    throw new Error(`${name} is empty — set it in Project Secrets and retry.`);
85	  }
86	  return v;
87	}
88	```
89	
90	Call `requireEnv("XERO_CLIENT_ID")`, `XERO_CLIENT_SECRET`, `XERO_SCOPES`, and
91	`TOKEN_ENC_KEY` at the top of every handler that uses them.
92	
93	### 2. Log the constructed authorize URL once
94	
95	You want a server log you can read when something is wrong, without ever
96	printing the secret value itself:
97	
98	```ts
99	console.log("[xero] authorize", {
100	  user_id: context.userId,
101	  client_id_len: process.env.XERO_CLIENT_ID?.length,
102	  redirect_uri: redirectUri,
103	  scope: process.env.XERO_SCOPES,
104	});
105	```
106	
107	If the next failure is `unauthorized_client` or `invalid redirect_uri`, this
108	line tells you *exactly* which value Xero rejected.
109	
110	### 3. Smoke-test `xeroStart` BEFORE clicking Connect
111	
112	Sign in as a test user, call the server function, and inspect the returned URL:
113	`client_id` must be a non-empty hex string, and `redirect_uri` must **exactly**
114	match one of the URIs registered in your Xero app (including scheme, host, and
115	path). If either is wrong, fix it here. Do not proceed to the popup — Xero's
116	errors past this point are far less useful than the URL you can read right now.
117	
118	### 4. Validate `TOKEN_ENC_KEY` decodes to exactly 32 bytes
119	
120	AES-256-GCM requires a **32-byte** key. A 32-character random string is NOT 32
121	bytes once base64-decoded (it's ~24), and `createCipheriv` throws
122	`Invalid key length` from deep inside `encrypt()` — which surfaces as an
123	opaque **500 on the callback**, *after* Xero has already consumed the
124	single-use `code`. The user sees "something went wrong" with no way to retry
125	without re-consenting, and no breadcrumb in the logs points at the key. In
126	multi-tenant this is worse: every customer who tries to connect hits the same
127	500 until you fix the key.
128	
129	Make `key()` self-validating and tolerant of either shape:
130	
131	```ts
132	// src/lib/server/crypto.server.ts
133	import { createCipheriv, createDecipheriv, createHash, randomBytes } from "node:crypto";
134	function key() {
135	  const k = process.env.TOKEN_ENC_KEY;
136	  if (!k) throw new Error("TOKEN_ENC_KEY is not set");
137	  const decoded = Buffer.from(k, "base64");
138	  if (decoded.length === 32) return decoded;                         // preferred shape
139	  if (k.length >= 32) return createHash("sha256").update(k, "utf8").digest();  // tolerant shim
140	  throw new Error("TOKEN_ENC_KEY too short — must be base64(32 bytes) or ≥32 chars");
141	}
142	```
143	
144	The SHA-256 branch is a one-way compatibility shim so a mis-generated secret
145	doesn't crash the app; do not rely on it as the *intended* path. Generation
146	guidance is in §1.
147	
148	---
149	
150	
151	## 1. Register your own Xero app
152	
153	In the Xero developer portal (developer.xero.com) create a **Web app** OAuth2 app
154	(confidential — it holds a secret, which your server functions can):
155	
156	- Grab **Client ID** and **Client Secret**.
157	- **Register BOTH redirect URIs**, exactly:
158	  - **Published:** `https://project--<project-id>.lovable.app/api/public/xero/callback`
159	  - **Preview:** `https://id-preview--<project-id>.lovable.app/api/public/xero/callback`
160	
161	  Tell the user to register both before first connect. The in-editor preview
162	  iframe host (`*.lovableproject.com`) and the dev-server origin (`localhost:8080`)
163	  are **NOT** valid redirect URIs — don't register them and don't send them.
164	  See the resolver in §3.
165	
166	- Decide your scopes (see **Scopes** next). You MUST include **`offline_access`**
167	  or Xero returns no refresh token. Do not ask the user what scopes to use - set
168	  the appropriate scopes for the integration to function.
169	
170	Store in Lovable Cloud secrets:
171	- `XERO_CLIENT_ID`, `XERO_CLIENT_SECRET`
172	- `XERO_SCOPES` (space-separated string)
173	- `TOKEN_ENC_KEY` — must base64-decode to exactly **32 bytes**. Call
174	  `generate_secret` with **`length: 44`** (the base64 length of 32 random
175	  bytes). The default 32-char length is the common mistake — it decodes to
176	  ~24 bytes and AES-256-GCM rejects it at runtime (see Pre-flight §4).
177	
178	You do **not** need a `XERO_REDIRECT_URI` secret — the resolver in §3 derives it
179	from the request origin so preview and published both work without juggling
180	environment-specific secrets. `SUPABASE_URL` and `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` are
181	already available via `process.env`. **Never** put per-user tokens in secrets —
182	they go in the database, encrypted. The refresh token **rotates on every
183	refresh**, so it must live in a **mutable** store (the database).
184	
185	> Read `process.env.*` **inside** `.handler()`, not at module scope. The Worker
186	> runtime injects env per-request; a module-level read returns `undefined`.
187	
188	## Scopes
189	
190	Scopes are **additive** — request the minimum and re-run consent to add more later.
191	You **cannot** widen scope on an existing token; the user must re-consent. To get
192	a refresh token at all you must include `offline_access`.
193	
194	(This table is identical to the single-account skill — keep the two in sync if
195	Xero's assignments change.)
196	
197	### User / OpenID scopes (identity)
198	
199	| Scope | Description |
200	| --- | --- |
201	| `offline_access` | required for a refresh token (offline connection) |
202	| `openid` | use the user's identity |
203	| `profile` | first name, last name, full name, Xero user id |
204	| `email` | email address |
205	
206	### Accounting API — prefer granular scopes
207	
208	Xero is replacing **broad** scopes with **granular** ones. Since **March 2026** all
209	new and existing Web/PKCE apps are assigned granular scopes; custom connections
210	since **29 April 2026**. Broad scopes keep working for apps that already used them
211	only until **September 2027**. **Always prefer the granular scopes** — use
212	`accounting.invoices`, NOT `accounting.transactions`.
213	
214	Deprecated broad scope → the granular scopes that replace it:
215	
216	| Deprecated (broad) | New granular scopes |
217	| --- | --- |
218	| `accounting.transactions` | `accounting.invoices`, `accounting.payments`, `accounting.banktransactions`, `accounting.manualjournals` |
219	| `accounting.transactions.read` | `accounting.invoices.read`, `accounting.payments.read`, `accounting.banktransactions.read`, `accounting.manualjournals.read` |
220	| `accounting.reports.read` | `accounting.reports.aged.read`, `accounting.reports.balancesheet.read`, `accounting.reports.banksummary.read`, `accounting.reports.budgetsummary.read`, `accounting.reports.executivesummary.read`, `accounting.reports.profitandloss.read`, `accounting.reports.trialbalance.read`, `accounting.reports.taxreports.read` |
221	
222	What each granular scope covers (read variants are the same but GET-only):
223	
224	| Scope | Grants |
225	| --- | --- |
226	| `accounting.invoices` | Invoices, CreditNotes, Quotes, PurchaseOrders, RepeatingInvoices, LinkedTransactions, Items |
227	| `accounting.payments` | Payments, BatchPayments, Overpayments, Prepayments |
228	| `accounting.banktransactions` | BankTransactions, BankTransfers |
229	| `accounting.manualjournals` | ManualJournals |
230	| `accounting.reports.*.read` | the matching report only (balancesheet, profitandloss, trialbalance, taxreports = GSTReport/BASReport, etc.) |
231	
232	### Accounting scopes that were never broad (use as-is)
233	
234	| Scope | Grants |
235	| --- | --- |
236	| `accounting.settings` / `accounting.settings.read` | Accounts, BrandingThemes, Currencies, Items, InvoiceReminders, Organisation, TaxRates, TrackingCategories, Users |
237	| `accounting.contacts` / `accounting.contacts.read` | Contacts, ContactGroups |
238	| `accounting.attachments` / `accounting.attachments.read` | attachments across most resources |
239	| `accounting.journals.read` | Journals (general ledger) |
240	| `accounting.budgets.read` | Budgets |
241	
242	**Read vs write — choose deliberately; you can't change it later without
243	re-consent.** The `.read` scopes are GET-only. If your app **creates or modifies**
244	anything (invoices, contacts, payments), you must request the **write** (non-`.read`)
245	scope for each. Shipping a read-only token and then calling `POST /Invoices` fails
246	at runtime with a 403 / insufficient-scope error — and because scopes **cannot be
247	widened on an existing token**, the only fix is to send every connected customer
248	back through the whole consent flow. So decide up front: read, write, or both.
249	
250	A typical invoicing SaaS therefore wants:
251	```
252	openid profile email offline_access accounting.contacts accounting.invoices
253	```
254	
255	Store this string in the `XERO_SCOPES` secret (space-separated). **Make the
256	`XERO_SCOPES` fallback in your shared Xero config match your intent** — an
257	all-`.read` default will silently hand every customer a read-only token if the
258	secret is missing, and your first write will 403.
259	
260	Source of truth (re-check periodically — dates and assignments change):
261	https://developer.xero.com/documentation/guides/oauth2/scopes
262	
263	## 2. Per-user token store (encrypted, RLS-locked)
264	
265	One row per (user, Xero tenant). Store the token set encrypted as JSON, plus the
266	selected `tenantId`. Add a short-lived flow table for the `state` handshake.
267	
268	```sql
269	-- xero_connections: one row per (your user, Xero org)
270	create table public.xero_connections (
271	  id            uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
272	  user_id       uuid not null references auth.users(id) on delete cascade,
273	  tenant_id     text not null,                 -- Xero org id
274	  tenant_name   text,
275	  token_set_enc text not null,                 -- AES-256-GCM ciphertext of the token set JSON
276	  status        text not null default 'active',-- 'active' | 'needs_reconnect'
277	  expires_at    timestamptz not null,
278	  created_at    timestamptz not null default now(),
279	  updated_at    timestamptz not null default now(),
280	  unique (user_id, tenant_id)
281	);
282	-- Only the service role (used inside server functions / server routes) may touch this.
283	-- Do NOT grant anon/authenticated — the browser must never read token_set_enc.
284	grant all on public.xero_connections to service_role;
285	alter table public.xero_connections enable row level security;
286	-- INTENTIONALLY no anon/authenticated policies. NEVER add a policy that lets the
287	-- logged-in user SELECT token_set_enc — tokens must never reach the browser.
288	
289	-- xero_oauth_flows: short-lived CSRF/state handshake between start and callback
290	create table public.xero_oauth_flows (
291	  state      text primary key,
292	  user_id    uuid not null references auth.users(id) on delete cascade,
293	  created_at timestamptz not null default now(),
294	  expires_at timestamptz not null
295	);
296	grant all on public.xero_oauth_flows to service_role;
297	alter table public.xero_oauth_flows enable row level security;  -- service-role only
298	```
299	
300	AES-256-GCM helpers using **Node `crypto`**. The blob is three parts
301	(`iv.tag.ciphertext`, base64) — this is correct for `node:crypto` GCM, where the
302	auth tag is exposed separately. Put helpers in a `.server.ts` file so they're
303	blocked from the client bundle.
304	
305	```ts
306	// src/lib/server/crypto.server.ts
307	import { createCipheriv, createDecipheriv, createHash, randomBytes } from "node:crypto";
308	
309	function key() {
310	  const k = process.env.TOKEN_ENC_KEY;
311	  if (!k) throw new Error("TOKEN_ENC_KEY is not set");
312	  const decoded = Buffer.from(k, "base64");
313	  if (decoded.length === 32) return decoded;
314	  if (k.length >= 32) return createHash("sha256").update(k, "utf8").digest();  // tolerant shim
315	  throw new Error("TOKEN_ENC_KEY too short — must be base64(32 bytes) or ≥32 chars");
316	}
317	
318	export function encrypt(plain: string) {
319	  const iv = randomBytes(12);
320	  const c = createCipheriv("aes-256-gcm", key(), iv);
321	  const ct = Buffer.concat([c.update(plain, "utf8"), c.final()]);
322	  const tag = c.getAuthTag();
323	  return `${iv.toString("base64")}.${tag.toString("base64")}.${ct.toString("base64")}`;
324	}
325	
326	export function decrypt(blob: string) {
327	  const [iv, tag, ct] = blob.split(".").map((s) => Buffer.from(s, "base64"));
328	  const d = createDecipheriv("aes-256-gcm", key(), iv);
329	  d.setAuthTag(tag);
330	  return Buffer.concat([d.update(ct), d.final()]).toString("utf8");
331	}
332	```
333	
334	Shared Xero config (client-safe constants only — secrets are read inside handlers):
335	
336	```ts
337	// src/lib/xero-config.ts
338	export const XERO = {
339	  authorize:   "https://login.xero.com/identity/connect/authorize",
340	  token:       "https://identity.xero.com/connect/token",
341	  connections: "https://api.xero.com/connections",
342	  api:         "https://api.xero.com/api.xro/2.0",
343	};
344	export const basicAuth = (id: string, secret: string) =>
345	  Buffer.from(`${id}:${secret}`).toString("base64");
346	export const randomState = () =>
347	  Buffer.from(crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(16)))
348	    .toString("base64")
349	    .replace(/\+/g, "-").replace(/\//g, "_").replace(/=+$/, "");
350	```
351	
352	---
353	
354	## 3. The redirect-URI resolver — read carefully
355	
356	> ### ⚠️ Three traps, all of which produce `invalid redirect_uri` or `unauthorized_client`
357	>
358	> **Trap A — `getRequest()` / `request.url` on the server returns
359	> `http://localhost:8080`.** Under the Worker runtime the inbound request you
360	> see inside a server function is on the internal dev host, not the public URL.
361	> Never derive `redirect_uri` from it inside `xeroStart`.
362	>
363	> **Trap B — In the Lovable editor, `window.location.origin` is
364	> `https://<project-id>.lovableproject.com`** (the iframe host), which is NOT
365	> a registered URI. Sending it raw produces `invalid redirect_uri` every time.
366	>
367	> **Trap C — A single `XERO_REDIRECT_URI` secret can't cover both preview and
368	> published.** If you set it to the published host the preview breaks (and
369	> vice-versa). Use the resolver below instead.
370	
371	Pass the client origin into `xeroStart`, then **map** unregistered hosts to the
372	matching registered URI:
373	
374	```ts
375	// src/lib/xero-redirect.ts
376	const PROJECT_ID = "<your-project-id>";
377	const PREVIEW   = `https://id-preview--${PROJECT_ID}.lovable.app`;
378	const PUBLISHED = `https://project--${PROJECT_ID}.lovable.app`;
379	const CALLBACK  = "/api/public/xero/callback";
380	
381	export function resolveRedirectUri(clientOrigin: string | undefined): string {
382	  const origin = (clientOrigin ?? "").trim();
383	  if (!origin) return `${PREVIEW}${CALLBACK}`;
384	  let host: string;
385	  try { host = new URL(origin).hostname; } catch { return `${PREVIEW}${CALLBACK}`; }
386	  // Editor iframe host — not registered with Xero.
387	  if (host.endsWith(".lovableproject.com")) return `${PREVIEW}${CALLBACK}`;
388	  // Local dev — not registered with Xero.
389	  if (host === "localhost" || host === "127.0.0.1") return `${PREVIEW}${CALLBACK}`;
390	  // Registered hosts pass through unchanged.
391	  if (origin.startsWith(PUBLISHED)) return `${PUBLISHED}${CALLBACK}`;
392	  if (origin.startsWith(PREVIEW))   return `${PREVIEW}${CALLBACK}`;
393	  // Custom domain or anything else → default to preview. Add an explicit
394	  // branch here if you ship a custom domain.
395	  return `${PREVIEW}${CALLBACK}`;
396	}
397	```
398	
399	Use the same resolver in **both** `xeroStart` (with the client-supplied origin)
400	and the callback route (with `new URL(request.url).origin` — which DOES work
401	correctly inside a server route because it's the actual public URL Xero hit).
402	
403	### `xeroStart` (consent kickoff)
404	
405	`state` is your CSRF defense and your link back to the initiating user. Persist
406	it server-side (the `xero_oauth_flows` row) — never trust it blind on return.
407	Use `requireSupabaseAuth` so the handler always has the calling user's id; this
408	is what scopes every stored token row to the right customer.
409	
410	```ts
411	// src/lib/xero-start.functions.ts
412	import { createServerFn } from "@tanstack/react-start";
413	import { requireSupabaseAuth } from "@/integrations/supabase/auth-middleware";
414	import { XERO, randomState } from "./xero-config";
415	import { resolveRedirectUri } from "./xero-redirect";
416	
417	export const xeroStart = createServerFn({ method: "POST" })
418	  .middleware([requireSupabaseAuth])
419	  .inputValidator((d: { origin?: string } | undefined) => ({ origin: d?.origin ?? "" }))
420	  .handler(async ({ data, context }) => {
421	    const { supabaseAdmin } = await import("@/integrations/supabase/client.server");
422	
423	    // Pre-flight: fail fast with a useful message.
424	    const clientId = process.env.XERO_CLIENT_ID;
425	    const scopes   = process.env.XERO_SCOPES;
426	    if (!clientId) throw new Error("XERO_CLIENT_ID is empty — set it in Project Secrets.");
427	    if (!scopes)   throw new Error("XERO_SCOPES is empty — set it in Project Secrets.");
428	
429	    const redirectUri = resolveRedirectUri(data.origin);
430	    console.log("[xero] authorize", {
431	      user_id: context.userId, client_id_len: clientId.length, redirectUri, scopes,
432	    });
433	
434	    const state = randomState();
435	    await supabaseAdmin.from("xero_oauth_flows").insert({
436	      state,
437	      user_id: context.userId,
438	      expires_at: new Date(Date.now() + 10 * 60_000).toISOString(),
439	    });
440	
441	    const url = new URL(XERO.authorize);
442	    url.searchParams.set("response_type", "code");
443	    url.searchParams.set("client_id", clientId);
444	    url.searchParams.set("redirect_uri", redirectUri);
445	    url.searchParams.set("scope", scopes);
446	    url.searchParams.set("state", state);
447	    return { url: url.toString() };
448	  });
449	```
450	
451	> **Why no PKCE here.** Your server functions can hold the client secret, so the
452	> standard confidential web-app flow is correct: the **secret** authenticates the
453	> token exchange and `state` covers CSRF. PKCE exists for public clients that
454	> *can't* keep a secret (mobile/SPA); bolting it onto a confidential server flow
455	> adds a `code_verifier` you'd have to persist across the two stateless
456	> invocations for no security gain. Don't use PKCE for this — secret + `state`.
457	
458	### Click handler — iframe rule
459	
460	> ### ⚠️ Open consent in a NEW TAB, opened SYNCHRONOUSLY in the click handler
461	>
462	> Xero's login refuses to be framed (`frame-ancestors`), so a same-frame
463	> redirect from the Lovable preview iframe shows a **blank white page** that
464	> looks like the button is broken. Detect the iframe and open a top-level tab.
465	> The `window.open()` call MUST happen *before* any `await`, or popup blockers
466	> will eat it. Always pass `window.location.origin` into `xeroStart` so the
467	> resolver maps it correctly.
468	
469	```tsx
470	import { useServerFn } from "@tanstack/react-start";
471	import { xeroStart } from "@/lib/xero-start.functions";
472	
473	const start = useServerFn(xeroStart);
474	async function onConnect() {
475	  const inIframe = window.self !== window.top;
476	  const popup = inIframe ? window.open("about:blank", "_blank") : null;  // sync!
477	  const { url } = await start({ data: { origin: window.location.origin } });
478	  if (popup) popup.location.href = url;
479	  else window.location.href = url;
480	}
481	```
482	
483	The published app at `<your-app>.lovable.app` is not iframed, so the same-tab
484	redirect works there — handle both. After OAuth, let TanStack Query's
485	refetch-on-focus refresh the connection list in the original tab.
486	
487	## 4. Callback — exchange, pick tenant(s), store
488	
489	Xero redirects the **browser** here with no app session. The handler lives at a
490	server route under `src/routes/api/public/` so it bypasses Lovable's published-site
491	auth gate; trust comes from the `state` row written by `xeroStart`, which carries
492	the originating `user_id`. Use the **same** `resolveRedirectUri` helper as in
493	`xeroStart` — the token-exchange `redirect_uri` MUST byte-match the one used to
494	start the flow.
495	
496	```ts
497	// src/routes/api/public/xero.callback.ts
498	import { createFileRoute } from "@tanstack/react-router";
499	import { XERO, basicAuth } from "@/lib/xero-config";
500	import { resolveRedirectUri } from "@/lib/xero-redirect";
501	
502	export const Route = createFileRoute("/api/public/xero/callback")({
503	  server: {
504	    handlers: {
505	      GET: async ({ request }) => {
506	        const u = new URL(request.url);
507	        const origin = u.origin;            // safe here — this IS the public URL Xero hit
508	        const back = (q: string) =>
509	          new Response(null, { status: 302, headers: { Location: `${origin}/xero${q}` } });
510	        try {
511	        const { supabaseAdmin } = await import("@/integrations/supabase/client.server");
512	        const { encrypt } = await import("@/lib/server/crypto.server");
513	
514	        const redirectUri = resolveRedirectUri(origin);
515	
516	        const code = u.searchParams.get("code");
517	        const state = u.searchParams.get("state");
518	        if (!code || !state) return back("?error=missing_params");
519	
520	        const { data: flow } = await supabaseAdmin
521	          .from("xero_oauth_flows").select("*").eq("state", state).maybeSingle();
522	        if (!flow || new Date(flow.expires_at as string) < new Date())
523	          return back("?error=bad_state");
524	
525	        // confidential flow: Basic auth with the secret
526	        const tokenRes = await fetch(XERO.token, {
527	          method: "POST",
528	          headers: {
529	            Authorization: `Basic ${basicAuth(process.env.XERO_CLIENT_ID!, process.env.XERO_CLIENT_SECRET!)}`,
530	            "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
531	          },
532	          body: new URLSearchParams({
533	            grant_type: "authorization_code",
534	            code,
535	            redirect_uri: redirectUri,
536	          }),
537	        });
538	        if (!tokenRes.ok) {
539	          console.error("[xero] token exchange failed", await tokenRes.text());
540	          return back("?error=token_exchange");
541	        }
542	        const tokenSet = await tokenRes.json();
543	
544	        // which orgs did this single consent authorize?
545	        const conns = await fetch(XERO.connections, {
546	          headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${tokenSet.access_token}` },
547	        }).then((r) => r.json()) as Array<{ tenantId: string; tenantName: string }>;
548	
549	        // A single consent can authorize MULTIPLE orgs that all SHARE ONE token set.
550	        // Don't assume conns[0]. Encrypt ONCE and write the IDENTICAL ciphertext to every
551	        // row — this lets refresh (§5) find and update all sibling rows together.
552	        const enc = encrypt(JSON.stringify(tokenSet));
553	        const expiresAt = new Date(Date.now() + tokenSet.expires_in * 1000).toISOString();
554	        for (const c of conns) {
555	          await supabaseAdmin.from("xero_connections").upsert(
556	            { user_id: flow.user_id, tenant_id: c.tenantId, tenant_name: c.tenantName,
557	              token_set_enc: enc, status: "active", expires_at: expiresAt },
558	            { onConflict: "user_id,tenant_id" },
559	          );
560	        }
561	        // single-use: consume state so a captured callback URL can't be replayed
562	        await supabaseAdmin.from("xero_oauth_flows").delete().eq("state", state);
563	        return back("?connected=1");
564	        } catch (e) {
565	          // Any throw past token_exchange MUST funnel into the same ?error/&detail
566	          // redirect — a bare 500 is unrecoverable here because Xero's `code` is
567	          // single-use and the user has to re-consent before you can read the next log.
568	          console.error("[xero] callback error", e);
569	          const detail = encodeURIComponent(e instanceof Error ? e.message : String(e));
570	          return back(`?error=callback&detail=${detail}`);
571	        }
572	      },
573	    },
574	  },
575	});
576	```
577	
578	## 5. Use it per request (refresh + persist rotation)
579	
580	The server runtime instantiates fresh each call, so "never cache a client" is
581	automatic. Refresh slightly early, rotate the refresh token, and update **all
582	sibling rows** that shared the old token set. Put this in a `.server.ts` helper
583	and call it from inside authenticated server-function handlers.
584	
585	```ts
586	// src/lib/server/xero-token.server.ts
587	import { XERO, basicAuth } from "@/lib/xero-config";
588	import { encrypt, decrypt } from "./crypto.server";
589	
590	export async function getAccessToken(userId: string, tenantId: string) {
591	  const { supabaseAdmin } = await import("@/integrations/supabase/client.server");
592	
593	  const { data: conn } = await supabaseAdmin.from("xero_connections")
594	    .select("*").eq("user_id", userId).eq("tenant_id", tenantId).single();
595	  if (!conn) throw new Error("User has not connected this Xero org");
596	
597	  let tokenSet = JSON.parse(decrypt(conn.token_set_enc));
598	
599	  if (new Date(conn.expires_at) <= new Date(Date.now() + 60_000)) {
600	    const res = await fetch(XERO.token, {
601	      method: "POST",
602	      headers: {
603	        Authorization: `Basic ${basicAuth(process.env.XERO_CLIENT_ID!, process.env.XERO_CLIENT_SECRET!)}`,
604	        "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
605	      },
606	      body: new URLSearchParams({
607	        grant_type: "refresh_token",
608	        refresh_token: tokenSet.refresh_token,
609	      }),
610	    });
611	    if (!res.ok) {
612	      // refresh token is dead → surface a reconnect state, don't loop forever
613	      await supabaseAdmin.from("xero_connections")
614	        .update({ status: "needs_reconnect" })
615	        .eq("user_id", userId).eq("token_set_enc", conn.token_set_enc);
616	      throw new Error("Xero refresh failed — prompt reconnect");
617	    }
618	    tokenSet = await res.json();                         // refresh_token ROTATES — must persist
619	    const enc = encrypt(JSON.stringify(tokenSet));
620	    const expiresAt = new Date(Date.now() + tokenSet.expires_in * 1000).toISOString();
621	    // CRITICAL multi-tenant trap: orgs that shared ONE token set all hold the SAME
622	    // (now-rotated, invalidated) refresh token. Update EVERY sibling row, matched on
623	    // the OLD ciphertext, or the others flip to needs_reconnect on their next call.
624	    await supabaseAdmin.from("xero_connections")
625	      .update({ token_set_enc: enc, expires_at: expiresAt, updated_at: new Date().toISOString() })
626	      .eq("user_id", userId).eq("token_set_enc", conn.token_set_enc);
627	  }
628	  return tokenSet.access_token as string;
629	}
630	```
631	
632	Example write inside an authenticated server function — note the explicit
633	`Xero-Tenant-Id` header (there is no implicit "current org"), `summarizeErrors=false`,
634	`unitdp=4`, and an idempotency key:
635	
636	```ts
637	// src/lib/xero-invoices.functions.ts
638	import { createServerFn } from "@tanstack/react-start";
639	import { requireSupabaseAuth } from "@/integrations/supabase/auth-middleware";
640	import { z } from "zod";
641	import { XERO } from "./xero-config";
642	
643	export const createInvoice = createServerFn({ method: "POST" })
644	  .middleware([requireSupabaseAuth])
645	  .inputValidator((d) => z.object({ tenantId: z.string(), invoice: z.unknown() }).parse(d))
646	  .handler(async ({ data, context }) => {
647	    const { getAccessToken } = await import("./server/xero-token.server");
648	    const accessToken = await getAccessToken(context.userId, data.tenantId);
649	
650	    const res = await fetch(`${XERO.api}/Invoices?unitdp=4&summarizeErrors=false`, {
651	      method: "POST",
652	      headers: {
653	        Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}`,
654	        "Xero-Tenant-Id": data.tenantId,                       // REQUIRED — wrong id writes to wrong books
655	        "Idempotency-Key": crypto.randomUUID(),
656	        "Content-Type": "application/json",
657	        Accept: "application/json",
658	      },
659	      body: JSON.stringify({ Invoices: [data.invoice] }),
660	    });
661	    if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`Xero invoice failed: ${res.status} ${await res.text()}`);
662	    return res.json();
663	  });
664	```
665	
666	## 6. Surface connection status (reconnect UX)
667	
668	A real dashboard has to *show* when a customer's connection has dropped. A failed
669	refresh flips `status` to `needs_reconnect`, and from then on every Xero call for
670	that customer throws until they reconnect — don't leave that invisible behind a
671	generic error. Expose a tiny **status** server function that returns only
672	non-sensitive metadata (never the token) and drive a banner from it.
673	
674	```ts
675	// src/lib/xero-status.functions.ts
676	import { createServerFn } from "@tanstack/react-start";
677	import { requireSupabaseAuth } from "@/integrations/supabase/auth-middleware";
678	
679	export const xeroConnections = createServerFn({ method: "GET" })
680	  .middleware([requireSupabaseAuth])
681	  .handler(async ({ context }) => {
682	    const { supabaseAdmin } = await import("@/integrations/supabase/client.server");
683	    const { data } = await supabaseAdmin.from("xero_connections")
684	      .select("tenant_id, tenant_name, status")
685	      .eq("user_id", context.userId);
686	    return data ?? [];
687	  });
688	```
689	
690	Frontend: read it (or refetch on focus) and show a reconnect CTA per row when
691	needed — the CTA runs the very same connect flow as the first-time hookup.
692	
693	Mark `needs_reconnect` *proactively*, not only after a failed refresh: a refresh
694	token unused for 60 days dies silently, so a low-traffic customer can read as
695	"connected" yet be broken. A scheduled keep-alive refresh (see gotchas) plus this
696	banner keeps the displayed state honest.
697	
698	Also read `?error=…&detail=…` from the URL on the post-callback landing page
699	(e.g. `/xero`) and render it in the connection UI. The callback (§4) redirects
700	every failure with those params; without surfacing them, a callback crash
701	looks identical to "not connected yet" and the user has no idea anything
702	happened.
703	
704	
705	## Date + money + tax normalisation
706	
707	- **Dates.** Calling REST directly, responses contain Xero's Microsoft-JSON date
708	  form (`/Date(1518685950940+0000)/`), which `new Date()` can't parse. Normalise
709	  incoming dates and emit ISO 8601; send dates as `YYYY-MM-DD`.
710	
711	  ```ts
712	  export function normalizeXeroDate(v: unknown): Date | null {
713	    if (v == null) return null;
714	    if (v instanceof Date) return isNaN(v.getTime()) ? null : v;
715	    if (typeof v === "string") {
716	      const m = v.match(/\/Date\((-?\d+)([+-]\d{4})?\)\//);
717	      const d = m ? new Date(Number(m[1])) : new Date(v);
718	      return isNaN(d.getTime()) ? null : d;
719	    }
720	    return null;
721	  }
722	  ```
723	
724	- **Money.** Send numbers, not formatted currency strings. Control rounding with
725	  the `unitdp` query param (up to 4). Postgres `numeric` comes back as strings via the
726	  Supabase client — convert to numbers before any client-side parse. `AmountDue` is a
727	  number.
728	
729	- **Tax is the org's, not yours.** Each connected organisation applies **its own**
730	  tax config, so `AmountDue` / `Total` may exceed the sum of your line amounts
731	  (e.g. 15% GST turns a 2495 line into 2869.25). The `UnitAmount` you posted is
732	  still correct — don't "fix" the discrepancy. Set `LineAmountTypes` (`Exclusive`,
733	  `Inclusive`, `NoTax`) deliberately if you need control. **In multi-tenant this
734	  varies per customer** — never hard-code a tax assumption; whatever each org
735	  returns is correct for that org.
736	
737	## Idempotency (avoid duplicate writes)
738	
739	Defend at two layers.
740	
741	**Layer 1 — app level:** dedup the logical action (a stable order id, a unique
742	constraint) so a double-submit never reaches Xero twice; upsert contacts by email
743	(see gotchas).
744	
745	**Layer 2 — Xero idempotency:** pass an `Idempotency-Key` header on mutating calls.
746	Xero caches the first response and replays it for repeats with the same key.
747	
748	Rules that bite:
749	- **Only POST/PUT/PATCH** honour the key; ignored on GET.
750	- **Max 128 chars.** A single `crypto.randomUUID()` (36) is plenty.
751	- **Keys expire after ~6 minutes** — they protect transient retries, not long-term
752	  dedup (that's Layer 1).
753	- **Same key + a different request → `400`.** Persist the key with the record and
754	  reuse the identical request when retrying.
755	- **Errors are cached too.** A keyed request that errored replays the cached error
756	  on retry; to recover, GET to check whether the resource was actually created,
757	  and if not, retry with a **new** key.
758	
759	When a user *re-submits* a previously **failed** order later, mint a **new** key —
760	but first GET to confirm no invoice was actually created, so you don't duplicate
761	one that silently succeeded.
762	
763	## Other Xero gotchas (these bite in production)
764	
765	- **`offline_access` is mandatory for refresh.** Omit it and the access token dies
766	  in ~30 min with no way to refresh — the user must re-consent.
767	- **Token lifetimes:** access token ~30 min; refresh token ~60 days and **rotates
768	  on every refresh**. Persist the new refresh token each time or the next refresh
769	  fails. A refresh token also dies if unused for 60 days — low-traffic customers
770	  hit this; consider a scheduled keep-alive refresh per connection.
771	- **Always send `Xero-Tenant-Id`** on every API call. There is no implicit "current
772	  org." With multiple connected orgs, the wrong id silently writes to the wrong
773	  company's books.
774	- **Re-fetch `/connections` periodically**, not just at callback — the connected
775	  org list can change as users add/remove orgs.
776	- **Contacts aren't deduplicated by email.** Posting an invoice with a new contact
777	  name creates a NEW contact each time. Look up by email/`ContactID` via
778	  `GET /Contacts` and reuse the id; otherwise you litter the org with duplicates.
779	- **Invoice status flow:** `DRAFT` → `SUBMITTED` → `AUTHORISED`; only `AUTHORISED`
780	  are real receivables and expose an online payment URL. Use `ACCREC` (sales) vs
781	  `ACCPAY` (bills) correctly.
782	- **Rate limits (per tenant):** ~60 calls/minute, 5,000/day, plus an app-wide
783	  per-minute cap and ~5 concurrent requests. On **429** read `Retry-After` and
784	  back off — multi-tenant apps hit this fast when looping over orgs. Spread/queue
785	  calls per tenant.
786	- **`summarizeErrors=false`** on batch creates returns per-item validation errors.
787	
788	### Lovable / TanStack Start gotchas
789	
790	- **The callback MUST live under `src/routes/api/public/`.** That prefix bypasses
791	  Lovable's published-site auth gate; anywhere else, Xero's redirect lands on a
792	  login wall and the connect silently fails.
793	- **`xeroStart` must be gated by `requireSupabaseAuth`.** It's the middleware that
794	  binds the OAuth flow to the calling user; an unauthenticated `xeroStart` would
795	  store tokens against no one (or worse, the wrong customer). Unlike the
796	  single-account skill, there is **no prototype escape hatch** here — without a
797	  user id the flow is broken by construction.
798	- **The `state` value MUST be persisted.** The server runtime is stateless —
799	  start and callback are separate invocations with no shared memory.
800	- **Never import `@/integrations/supabase/client.server` at module scope of a
801	  `*.functions.ts` or route file.** Top-level code in those files ships to the
802	  client bundle (only handler bodies are stripped). Load it inside the handler.
803	- **`process.env.*` is server-only and must be read inside the handler**, not at
804	  module scope. A module-level read returns `undefined` under the Worker runtime.
805	- **`getRequest()` / `request.url` inside a server function returns
806	  `http://localhost:8080`** under the Worker runtime — useless for `redirect_uri`.
807	  Pass `window.location.origin` from the client into `xeroStart` and run it
808	  through `resolveRedirectUri`. (It IS safe to read `new URL(request.url).origin`
809	  inside the `/api/public/xero/callback` server route — that's a real public URL.)
810	- **`window.location.origin` in the editor is `*.lovableproject.com`** — also
811	  not registered. Always run it through `resolveRedirectUri`.
812	- **Service-role key never goes to the browser.** Surface only derived data
813	  (connection metadata, invoices) through your own server functions; the token
814	  tables have RLS with no anon/authenticated policies and no grants to those roles.
815	- **`npm:xero-node`** for read/write API calls is optional and must bundle cleanly
816	  for the Worker runtime — the `fetch`-based OAuth flow above avoids the dependency.
817	
818	## Security checklist (do not skip)
819	
820	- **Encrypt** the stored token set at rest; the key (`TOKEN_ENC_KEY`) lives in
821	  Lovable Cloud secrets, the rotating tokens in the DB.
822	- **Verify `state`** on every callback against the persisted flow row (CSRF), and
823	  **delete it after use** so a captured callback URL can't be replayed.
824	- **`xeroStart` and every other Xero server function require the user's session**
825	  via `requireSupabaseAuth`. Only the callback server route is public, and it only
826	  acts on a valid, unexpired, single-use `state`.
827	- **RLS on token tables with no user policies** — only the service role touches
828	  them. No `GRANT` to `anon` or `authenticated`.
829	- **Never expose tokens or the service-role key to the browser** or any
830	  client-readable storage.
831	- On a failed refresh, **mark the connection `needs_reconnect` and prompt
832	  re-connect** — don't silently retry forever.
833	- Keep all Xero access in **one place** (`xero-config.ts` + `xero-token.server.ts`
834	  + `xero-redirect.ts`) so paths, tenant handling, and gotchas live together.
835	
836	## Don't want to hand-roll it?
837	
838	Managed token-vault platforms (**Nango**, **Composio**, etc.) implement per-user
839	OAuth + refresh + encryption for Xero and many other providers, and you call out
840	to them from a server function. For a multi-provider SaaS this is often the
841	pragmatic choice.
842	
843	## Verify
844	
845	0. **Smoke-test before clicking Connect.** Sign in as a test user, call
846	   `xeroStart`, and inspect the returned URL — `client_id` non-empty, `redirect_uri`
847	   exactly matches a URI registered in your Xero app. Fix before touching the popup.
848	0.5. **Force an encryption failure once.** Temporarily set `TOKEN_ENC_KEY` to
849	   a 16-char string and click Connect. Confirm the post-callback page shows
850	   `?error=callback&detail=…` (with a readable message) rather than a blank
851	   500 — this proves both the callback try/catch (§4) and the surfacing UI
852	   (§6) actually wire failures back to the user. Restore the real key
853	   afterwards.
854	1. Two app users each connect a **different** Xero org; confirm two distinct token
855	   rows and that each user's writes land in their own org (check `Xero-Tenant-Id`).
856	2. Force a near-expiry token and confirm transparent refresh **and** that the
857	   rotated refresh token is persisted to every sibling row.
858	3. Revoke the app in one user's Xero and confirm your app surfaces a reconnect
859	   state (`needs_reconnect`) instead of looping on failed refreshes.
860	4. Post the same invoice twice with the same `Idempotency-Key` and confirm only
861	   one invoice is created.
862	5. Hit the callback route directly in a browser and confirm it isn't blocked by a
863	   login wall (i.e. it actually lives under `/api/public/`).
864	6. Confirm `xeroStart` rejects an unauthenticated caller (401 from middleware).
865	7. If your app writes to Xero, confirm a `POST` (e.g. create invoice) succeeds
866	   rather than 403 — proof tokens carry write scopes, not just `.read`.
867	8. Flip a connection to `needs_reconnect` and confirm the per-row banner appears
868	   and its CTA re-runs the connect flow.
869
```
</details></tool-use>
